startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 60-year-old diabetic patient presents with progressive oedema and declining renal function. Apply your knowledge of glomerular filtration and tubular function to guide diagnosis and management.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly applied renal physiology principles including GFR, tubular reabsorption, and the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system to this clinical scenario.
You showed some understanding but missed important concepts. Review GFR determinants, proximal tubular function, and ADH mechanisms.
Review the fundamentals of renal physiology: GFR (125 mL/min), Starling forces, proximal tubular reabsorption, and the juxtaglomerular apparatus.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 60-year-old man with a 20-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with progressive bilateral pedal oedema, facial puffiness, and frothy urine for the past 3 months. His blood pressure is 160/100 mmHg.

Laboratory findings:

  • Serum creatinine: 3.2 mg/dL (elevated)
  • Estimated GFR: 25 mL/min (normal ~125 mL/min)
  • Urine protein: 3.5 g/24 hours (nephrotic range)
  • Serum albumin: 2.1 g/dL (low)

The normal GFR is approximately 125 mL/min (180 litres/day). This patient's GFR is severely reduced to 25 mL/min. Consider what determines GFR.

branchingQuestion.question: GFR is determined by the net filtration pressure across the glomerular capillaries. Which of the following correctly describes the forces involved?

alternatives.text: Glomerular hydrostatic pressure favours filtration, while glomerular oncotic pressure opposes filtration

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: GFR depends on net filtration pressure = glomerular hydrostatic pressure (favouring filtration, ~60 mmHg) minus glomerular oncotic pressure (opposing filtration, ~32 mmHg) minus Bowman's capsule hydrostatic pressure (opposing, ~18 mmHg). Net filtration pressure ≈ 10 mmHg.

alternatives.text: Oncotic pressure in Bowman's capsule is the main force driving filtration

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Under normal conditions, Bowman's capsule has negligible oncotic pressure because the filtrate is essentially protein-free. The main driving force is glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure (~60 mmHg), generated by cardiac output and afferent arteriolar tone.

alternatives.text: GFR is solely determined by renal blood flow and is independent of pressure gradients

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: While renal blood flow affects GFR, filtration specifically depends on the balance of Starling forces across the glomerular capillary wall. Autoregulation maintains GFR relatively constant over a range of blood pressures (80-180 mmHg) through myogenic and tubuloglomerular feedback mechanisms.

params.text:
Tubular Function Assessment

Despite the reduced GFR, the patient is producing 2.5 L of urine per day (polyuria). In a healthy kidney, the proximal convoluted tubule reabsorbs approximately 65-70% of filtered sodium and water.

The patient's urine osmolality is only 280 mOsm/kg (similar to plasma), suggesting impaired concentration ability.

ADH (antidiuretic hormone) normally acts on the collecting duct to increase water reabsorption by inserting aquaporin-2 channels into the apical membrane.

In diabetic nephropathy, damage to the medullary interstitium disrupts the concentration gradient needed for ADH to work effectively.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient is started on an ACE inhibitor. This drug class acts on the renin-angiotensin system. The juxtaglomerular apparatus regulates renin secretion. Which cells secrete renin?

alternatives.text: Juxtaglomerular (granular) cells in the wall of the afferent arteriole

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Juxtaglomerular (granular) cells are modified smooth muscle cells in the afferent arteriolar wall that synthesise and secrete renin. The JGA also includes the macula densa (senses tubular NaCl) and extraglomerular mesangial cells. ACE inhibitors block conversion of angiotensin I to II, reducing efferent arteriolar tone and intraglomerular pressure.

alternatives.text: Macula densa cells in the distal convoluted tubule

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: The macula densa senses NaCl concentration in the tubular fluid and signals to the juxtaglomerular cells to regulate renin release (tubuloglomerular feedback). However, the macula densa itself does not secrete renin — that is the function of juxtaglomerular (granular) cells.

alternatives.text: Podocytes on the visceral layer of Bowman's capsule

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Podocytes form the visceral layer of Bowman's capsule and are part of the glomerular filtration barrier with their foot processes and slit diaphragms. They do not secrete renin. Renin is secreted by juxtaglomerular (granular) cells in the afferent arteriolar wall.

params.text:
Key Learning Points

  • Normal GFR: ~125 mL/min or 180 L/day; determined by net filtration pressure (Starling forces)
  • Net filtration pressure: Glomerular hydrostatic pressure (favours) minus oncotic pressure (opposes) minus Bowman's capsule pressure (opposes)
  • Proximal tubule: Reabsorbs 65-70% of filtered Na+ and water; site of glucose and amino acid reabsorption
  • ADH mechanism: Acts on collecting duct V2 receptors → cAMP → insertion of aquaporin-2 channels → increased water reabsorption
  • JGA components: Juxtaglomerular (granular) cells (secrete renin), macula densa (NaCl sensor), extraglomerular mesangial cells
  • ACE inhibitors: Block angiotensin I→II conversion, reduce efferent arteriolar tone, lower intraglomerular pressure — renoprotective in diabetic nephropathy
